preamble: The transformation from a voiceless bilabial plosive /p/ to a close front rounded vowel /y/ represents a significant phonetic shift. This change involves the complete vocalization of a consonant, moving from a stop articulation at the lips to a high, front, and rounded vowel produced in the oral cavity. Such a transformation typically entails a series of intermediate phonetic processes, including lenition (weakening of the consonant), palatalization (shift towards a palatal articulation), and ultimately vocalization, often conditioned by adjacent front vowels.

examples.note: Proto-Oti-Volta *p shifted to /y/ in Konni, particularly when followed by front vowels (/i/, /e/, or /ɛ/). This shift is observed both word-initially and word-medially.

In Ful (also known as Fula), a morphophonological process for forming plurals involves the alternation of initial consonants. This includes a change from /p/ to /y/ (or /w/) in certain plural forms of nouns belonging to the personal class.

sources: Naden, T. (1995). The Case of the 'Missing' Kɔnni P. Journal of West African Languages, 25(2), 15-28.
Sapir, E. (1921). Language: An Introduction to the Study of Speech. Harcourt, Brace and Company.
